title: Materialism
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Materialism
summary: Scripture consistently warns that the love of money chokes spiritual life, and calls believers to find their security and identity in God rather than in what they accumulate.
body:
Jesus speaks about money more than almost any other subject, and his diagnosis is severe: you cannot serve both God and money [verse:matt.6.24]. This is not a warning against having possessions but against being possessed by them. The issue is not wealth itself but the gravitational pull it exerts on the human heart.

The parable of the rich fool illustrates the danger. A man whose barns overflow plans to relax and enjoy his abundance — but God calls him a fool because his life will be demanded that very night [verse:luke.12.16-21]. His error was not success but a soul that stored up things for itself instead of being rich toward God.

Paul gives Timothy a sharper formulation: the love of money is a root of all kinds of evil, and some who have pursued it have wandered from the faith and pierced themselves with many griefs [verse:1tim.6.10]. The antidote is contentment — a settled confidence that godliness with enough is genuine gain [verse:1tim.6.6-8].

The writer of Hebrews echoes this directly: keep your life free from the love of money and be content with what you have, because God himself has promised never to leave or forsake you [verse:heb.13.5]. The deepest cure for materialism is not less stuff but a bigger God — one whose presence satisfies in a way that accumulation never can.

verses.annotation: Jesus' declaration that serving God and money simultaneously is impossible — one will always displace the other.
The parable of the rich fool who stored up earthly wealth but neglected his standing before God.
The love of money as a root of all kinds of evil, leading some to wander from faith.
Godliness with contentment presented as true gain, reframing the definition of wealth.
A command to reject the love of money grounded in God's promise of his own unfailing presence.
The Preacher's observation that whoever loves money never has enough — wealth does not satisfy.
The rich young ruler walks away sorrowful because his possessions had a grip his faith could not break.

scholars.quote: Keener emphasizes that Jesus' teaching on money and God in Matthew 6 uses the language of slave ownership — mammon is personified as a rival master demanding total allegiance.
scholars.source: A Commentary on the Gospel of Matthew (Keener, 1999)

scholars.quote: Towner argues that Paul's counsel to Timothy treats contentment not as passive resignation but as an active spiritual discipline that redefines prosperity in terms of godliness rather than accumulation.
scholars.source: The Letters to Timothy and Titus (Towner, 2006)

scholars.quote: Fox reads Ecclesiastes 5:10 as the Preacher's empirical finding: wealth structurally cannot satisfy because the desire for more always outpaces what one has.
